content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 7, 1988

Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
350 McAllister Street, Room 6000
San Francisco, California 94102

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-88-221

Dear Mr. Roche:

You have written requesting the Commission's views
regarding an opinion request pending with your office. To the
extent that the issues raised in your letter relate to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/ they
appear to arise from the provisions of Government Code Section
89001. This section was recently amended by the voters on
June 7 when they adopted Proposition 73.

The Commission has issued two interim advice letters on
this subject. Copies are enclosed for your use. The
Commission will address this issue further at its July 26,
1988, meeting. We will keep you apprised of the outcome.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: Robert B. Leidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

REL:ld
Enclosure

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.

28 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

State of California
DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE

350 McALLISTER STREET, ROOM 6000
SAN FRANCISCO 94102
(415) 557-2544

June 13, 1988

(415) 557-1586


John H. Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Calif  95816

Dear Mr. Larson:

Opinion No. 88-502

Our opinion has been requested on the following questions:

     1.  May a legislator send unsolicited information about
voter registration to his or her constituents?

     2.  May a legislator send unsolicited voter registration
affidavits to his or her constituents?

     3.  May a legislator have voter registration affidavits
available in either the Capitol office or a district office for
members of the public?

     4.  May the Assembly authorize its employees to become
deputy registrars and to register qualified citizens in Assembly
offices during regular working hours?

You are invited to submit whatever views you may have on the
question presented. To be of the most aid to me in preparing a
proposed response, views should reach me by July 1, 1988.
However, views received after that date and until final approval
of the opinion by the Attorney General will still be considered.

Very truly yours,

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

CLAYTON P. ROCHE
Deputy Attorney General

CPR:is
Encl.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 16, 1988


Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
350 McAllister Street, Room 6000
San Francisco, CA  94102

Re:  88-221

Dear Mr. Roche:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on June 15, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Robert Leidigh, an attorney in
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice.  If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed.  If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,


Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel
